title: China's shipbuilders spearhead green transition as IMO sets 2050 net-zero target - Anhui News
description:
Hengli has built a comprehensive green industrial chain ranging from core components to entire vessel manufacturing -- and is capable of producing dual-fuel ships powered by liquefied natural gas (LNG), methanol, ammonia and other options.
In the global push to combat climate change, the shipping industry, which is responsible for most of the world trade volume, faces mounting pressure to slash its carbon footprint.
The International Maritime Organization (IMO) solidified this urgency in April 2025 by mandating its net-zero framework -- targeting carbon neutrality for international shipping by 2050.
Against this backdrop, China's shipbuilding sector is emerging as a pivotal force, leveraging both innovation and scale to accelerate the transition toward green vessels.
Produced by Xinhua Global Service
text:
DALIAN, Jan. 25 (Xinhua) -- At a green ship development conference held in October last year in Dalian, a coastal city in northeast China's Liaoning Province and a key shipbuilding hub, Hengli Heavy Industry Group Co., Ltd. showcased its advancements in low-carbon vessel technology, drawing attention from European and Asian shipowners.

Hengli has built a comprehensive green industrial chain ranging from core components to entire vessel manufacturing -- and is capable of producing dual-fuel ships powered by liquefied natural gas (LNG), methanol, ammonia and other options.

In the global push to combat climate change, the shipping industry, which is responsible for most of the world trade volume, faces mounting pressure to slash its carbon footprint. The International Maritime Organization (IMO) solidified this urgency in April 2025 by mandating its net-zero framework -- targeting carbon neutrality for international shipping by 2050.

Against this backdrop, China's shipbuilding sector is emerging as a pivotal force, leveraging both innovation and scale to accelerate the transition toward green vessels.

Produced by Xinhua Global Service

name: IMO & Shipping
title: Understanding IMO NZF concerns key to moving forward: Dominguez - Seatrade Maritime
description:
IMO Secretary General Arsenio Dominguez called for member states to find a compromise deal that will allow the maritime industry to move forward with decarbonisation.
Dominguez added that the one of the main questions that is asked of the IMO is about its procedures and he explained that the extraordinary session had not voted, but had been suspended.
ÔÇťThe committee will decide when to reactivate it [the extraordinary session], but the committee continues to meet and the work continues within the remit,ÔÇŁ added Dominguez.
Dominguez rejected the view that the US remained in the IMO because President Donald Trump wanted to kill off the IMOÔÇÖs NZF.
[The US] recognised the value for this global sector in how we have made shipping more secure, more environmentally friendly and more sustainable,ÔÇŁ said Dominguez.
text:
IMO Secretary General Arsenio Dominguez called for member states to find a compromise deal that will allow the maritime industry to move forward with decarbonisation.

Last October was meant to see the Net Zero Framework (NZF) approved by MEPC 83, in April, finally enter into force following an extraordinary session of MEPC in October, but heavy lobbying by the US and Saudi delegations led to a decision to delay a vote on the NZF for one year.

According to Dominguez: ÔÇťOur role right now is taking into account the global climate in relation to multilateralism and negotiations on climate change and the outcomes of COP30 in Brazil, our focus is on the substance [of what we have on the table].ÔÇŁ

The Secretary General insists that the way forward for the regulatory body is to take into account some of the concerns that have been raised by member states, including the two major issues of how money collected from the industry will be dispersed, and the regulations around LNG and biofuels.

It is understood that many feel that LNG regulations are too stringent and that the gas can be a transitional fuel, but that the penalties imposed on the methane rich LNG would mean it is not economically viable.

Some owners believe that with no other fuel currently available, over-pricing LNG would be detrimental to the industry. Others argue that the way forward is to improve the efficiency of ships with existing technology, such as Mewis ducts, air lubrication and wind power along with weather routing.

Dominguez added that the one of the main questions that is asked of the IMO is about its procedures and he explained that the extraordinary session had not voted, but had been suspended.

ÔÇťThe committee will decide when to reactivate it [the extraordinary session], but the committee continues to meet and the work continues within the remit,ÔÇŁ added Dominguez.

IMO accepts that it does not have unanimity on some elements of the NZF, in fact Dominguez says there are three camps, those that are in favour of the framework, those who have some issues, but can live with it and those that have more concerns.

Nevertheless, Dominguez emphasised that IMO has, ÔÇťA strategy that was agreed back in 2023, and even during the last assembly, the objectives of the strategy were reiterated.ÔÇŁ

IMO has a mandate to protect the environment, including mitigating atmospheric pollution, so the work of the organisation is to find compromises, while ÔÇťrespecting all the different viewsÔÇŁ.

Earlier this month the Trump administration announced that it would withdraw from 66 international organisations, including the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change. It did not, however, withdraw from the IMO.

Dominguez rejected the view that the US remained in the IMO because President Donald Trump wanted to kill off the IMOÔÇÖs NZF.

ÔÇťIf you have the opportunity to listen to their statement at the assembly of the organisation in December, it was very supportive of all the work that the organisation carries out. [The US] recognised the value for this global sector in how we have made shipping more secure, more environmentally friendly and more sustainable,ÔÇŁ said Dominguez.

title: IMO Agrees on Guidance to Reduce Ship Underwater Noise, Extends Experience Phase
description:
IMO Agrees on Guidance to Reduce Ship Underwater Noise, Extends Experience Phase As a meeting of the International Maritime Organizations (IMO) Sub-Committee on Ship Design and Construction (SDC 12) closed Friday, the Clean Arctic Alliance welcomed the clear guidance on how the IMO must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts, and the recommendation of a two year extension of the three-year "experience building phase" (EBP).
"During this weeks IMO meeting, member states agreed on clear guidance on how we must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts during the proposed extension of the experience building phase.
A paper submitted to SDC 12 by WWF provides new maps that show, for the first time, the migratory corridors of Arctic whales across the Arctic Ocean.
The WWF paper outlines how IMO Member States should consider adopting shipping measures in the Arctic that build on the practice within IMO to safeguard whales from the adverse impacts of shipping.
Dumbrille's recommendations include acknowledging the irreversible impacts of underwater noise caused by LNG tanker traffic on marine mammals behaviours and habitat, and opposing LNG traffic in critical ecological marine areas because of the underwater noise pollution it causes.
text:
IMO Agrees on Guidance to Reduce Ship Underwater Noise, Extends Experience Phase

As a meeting of the International Maritime Organizations (IMO) Sub-Committee on Ship Design and Construction (SDC 12) closed Friday, the Clean Arctic Alliance welcomed the clear guidance on how the IMO must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts, and the recommendation of a two year extension of the three-year "experience building phase" (EBP). The information is reported by Hellenic Shipping News.

During SDC 12, Member States finalized a list of key technical aspects for reducing underwater noise from shipping, which will inform future policy considerations by the Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC) during the proposed two-year extension of the Experience Building Phase, and will be initially considered by MEPC 84 in April.

The Clean Arctic Alliance, made up of 24 not-for-profit organisations, is calling on member states to ensure the IMO continues to push for progress and action in reducing underwater noise from shipping, especially in Arctic waters where the levels of underwater noise are anticipated to nearly quadruple by 2030.

"During this weeks IMO meeting, member states agreed on clear guidance on how we must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts during the proposed extension of the experience building phase. At the two forthcoming MEPC meetings this year, we hope to pursue policy that will move beyond voluntary guidance", said Sarah Bobbe, Senior Manager, Arctic Program at Ocean Conservancy.

For many marine organisms sound is the most important means of communication. Under water vision is restricted and without good hearing ability, elementary functions such as navigating, finding prey and partners can be hampered.

"Much of the Arctic has been free of anthropogenic (human-sourced) sound for a long time, but with increased human activity, Arctic waters are becoming more noisy every year", said Bobbe. "An important source of noise is shipping, specifically from propellers and engines."

A paper submitted to SDC 12 by WWF provides new maps that show, for the first time, the migratory corridors of Arctic whales across the Arctic Ocean. Marine mammal migratory corridors are specified in both the URN Guidelines and the Polar Code as areas of special concern.

"By putting migratory corridors on the map for these three whale species that we know to be especially sensitive to underwater noise, we hope to support the shipping community to take necessary measures to safeguard them", said Melanie Lancaster, Senior Specialist, Arctic species conservation for WWFs Global Arctic Programme.

The WWF paper outlines how IMO Member States should consider adopting shipping measures in the Arctic that build on the practice within IMO to safeguard whales from the adverse impacts of shipping. Mandatory ship reporting systems and recommendatory seasonal areas to be avoided have been adopted for North Atlantic right whales along the United States east coast.

Andrew Dumbrille, Co-Founder and Director of Equal Routes, highlighted parallels with the Gulf of California. "Our recommendations to SDC 12, and to the forthcoming discussions at MEPC 84, for the Gulf of California are equally important for the Arctic as both regions could see devastating impacts from future increased shipping, especially from LNG tankers", he said.

Dumbrille's recommendations include acknowledging the irreversible impacts of underwater noise caused by LNG tanker traffic on marine mammals behaviours and habitat, and opposing LNG traffic in critical ecological marine areas because of the underwater noise pollution it causes.

text:
As a meeting of the International Maritime OrganizationÔÇÖs (IMO) Sub-Committee on Ship Design and Construction (SDC 12) closed Friday, the Clean Arctic Alliance welcomed the clear guidance on how the IMO must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts, and the recommendation of a two year extension of the three-year ÔÇťexperience building phaseÔÇŁ (EBP).

During SDC 12, Member States finalized a list of key technical aspects for reducing underwater noise from shipping, which will inform future policy considerations by the Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC) during the proposed two-year extension of the Experience Building Phase, and will be initially considered by MEPC 84 in April.

The Clean Arctic Alliance, made up of 24 not-for-profit organisations, is calling on member states to ensure the IMO continues to push for progress and action in reducing underwater noise from shipping, especially in Arctic waters where the levels of underwater noise are anticipated to nearly quadruple by 2030.

ÔÇťDuring this weekÔÇÖs IMO meeting, member states agreed on clear guidance on how we must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts during the proposed extension of the experience building phase. At the two forthcoming MEPC meetings this year, we hope to pursue policy that will move beyond voluntary guidanceÔÇŁ, said Sarah Bobbe, Senior Manager, Arctic Program at Ocean Conservancy, a member of both the Clean Arctic Alliance and the Clean Shipping Coalition. ÔÇťSo far, this work has not kept pace relative to the growing threats faced by regions experiencing increased shipping traffic, such as the Arctic, underscoring the need for parallel policy advancement through the IMOÔÇÖs Marine Environmental Protection Committee in the coming months.ÔÇŁ

For many marine organisms sound is the most important means of communication. Under water vision is restricted and without good hearing ability, elementary functions such as navigating, finding prey and partners can be hampered. This is of particular importance for marine mammals, and also for fish and even invertebrates.

ÔÇťMuch of the Arctic has been free of anthropogenic (human-sourced) sound for a long time, but with increased human activity, Arctic waters are becoming more noisy every yearÔÇŁ, said Bobbe. ÔÇťAn important source of noise is shipping, specifically from propellers and engines. Since shipping has increased substantially during recent decades, underwater noise is a growing problem, contributing to serious impact on the Arctic ecosystem.ÔÇŁ

Key IMO Papers:

A paper submitted to SDC 12 by WWF and presented during a lunchtime side event, SDC 12-INF.17 ÔÇô Spatio-temporal information on Arctic whale migration routes for consideration in voyage planning and other measures to reduce the impacts of underwater radiated noise on marine life provides new maps that show, for the first time, the migratory corridors of Arctic whales across the Arctic Ocean.

Marine mammal migratory corridors are specified in both the URN Guidelines and the Polar Code as areas of special concern because they contain high concentrations of animals sensitive to impacts of shipping. The maps provide mariners with new information on where they need to take measures in migratory months, which in the Arctic, are in spring and autumn. Operational measures, such as rerouting and restricting speed, can reduce impacts on migrating whales, and these measures go hand in hand with technical measures to quieten ships through new builds and modifications. WWF invites mariners to use the maps in voyage planning, and urges member states and the IMO to implement location-specific rules and recommendations to reduce impacts of underwater noise in migratory corridors.

ÔÇťBy putting migratory corridors on the map for these three whale species that we know to be especially sensitive to underwater noise, we hope to support the shipping community to take necessary measures to safeguard themÔÇŁ, said Melanie Lancaster, Senior Specialist, Arctic species conservation for WWFÔÇÖs Global Arctic Programme and member of the Clean Arctic Alliance. ÔÇťFrom the maps, we see that whales migrate impressive distances between their summer and winter habitats, some swimming thousands of kilometres across national borders and even into the high seas of the Central Arctic Ocean. It will take international cooperation among states, ship operators, shipping companies and IMO to ensure their safe and quiet passage.ÔÇŁ

The WWF paper outlines how IMO Member States should consider adopting shipping measures in the Arctic that build on the practice within IMO to safeguard whales from the adverse impacts of shipping.

Mandatory ship reporting systems and recommendatory seasonal areas to be avoided have been adopted for North Atlantic right whales along the United States east coast and recommendatory speed restrictions have been adopted as an Associated Protective Measure (APM) for whales in the North-Western Mediterranean Sea Particularly Sensitive Sea Area (PSSA). The paper provides the necessary information for similar preventative measures to be adopted to safeguard whales in the Arctic.

A paper submitted to SDC 12 by the Clean Shipping Coalition ÔÇô SDC 12/8/3 and sent onto MEPC 84 for consideration, shares the findings of a study on the potential underwater radiated noise (URN) impacts of LNG development on marine mammals in the Gulf of California. The paper examines the potential effects of URN from LNG tanker traffic on migratory and resident whales, as well as related impacts on air quality and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.

The proposed LNG terminals and the resulting increase in LNG tanker traffic are expected to pose serious threats to the Gulf of CaliforniaÔÇÖs unique biodiversity and conservation areas, including those designated as a UNESCO ÔÇťWorld Heritage SiteÔÇŁ and ÔÇťImportant Marine Mammal AreasÔÇŁ. These developments increase the risks of air, water, and underwater noise pollution. Given the Gulf of CaliforniaÔÇÖs critical ecological roleÔÇöparticularly as a habitat for resident and migratory marine megafaunaÔÇöa precautionary approach to industrial development is essential. It is increasingly evident that the regionÔÇÖs ecological integrity is incompatible with the scale and nature of heavy marine traffic associated with proposed LNG facilities.

Many regions, such as in the Arctic and the Gulf of California in Mexico, share similar potential impacts from underwater noise, and both have seen low shipping traffic in the past. Shipping traffic has been increasing in the last decade in the Arctic, while in the Gulf of California the developing LNG infrastructure will bring unprecedented vessel traffic to a marine area with enormous ecological value, in particular for marine mammals.

name: International Maritime Organization
title: GLOBALink | China's shipbuilders spearhead green transition as IMO sets 2050 net-zero target
description:
Hengli has built a comprehensive green industrial chain ranging from core components to entire vessel manufacturing -- and is capable of producing dual-fuel ships powered by liquefied natural gas (LNG), methanol, ammonia and other options.
In the global push to combat climate change, the shipping industry, which is responsible for most of the world trade volume, faces mounting pressure to slash its carbon footprint.
The International Maritime Organization (IMO) solidified this urgency in April 2025 by mandating its net-zero framework -- targeting carbon neutrality for international shipping by 2050.
Against this backdrop, China's shipbuilding sector is emerging as a pivotal force, leveraging both innovation and scale to accelerate the transition toward green vessels.
Produced by Xinhua Global Service
text:
DALIAN, Jan. 25 (Xinhua) -- At a green ship development conference held in October last year in Dalian, a coastal city in northeast China's Liaoning Province and a key shipbuilding hub, Hengli Heavy Industry Group Co., Ltd. showcased its advancements in low-carbon vessel technology, drawing attention from European and Asian shipowners.

Hengli has built a comprehensive green industrial chain ranging from core components to entire vessel manufacturing -- and is capable of producing dual-fuel ships powered by liquefied natural gas (LNG), methanol, ammonia and other options.

In the global push to combat climate change, the shipping industry, which is responsible for most of the world trade volume, faces mounting pressure to slash its carbon footprint. The International Maritime Organization (IMO) solidified this urgency in April 2025 by mandating its net-zero framework -- targeting carbon neutrality for international shipping by 2050.

Against this backdrop, China's shipbuilding sector is emerging as a pivotal force, leveraging both innovation and scale to accelerate the transition toward green vessels.

Produced by Xinhua Global Service

text:
Ryan Ba┬şchoo

Lead Ed┬şi┬ştor - News┬şgath┬şer┬şing

ryan.ba┬şchoo@cnc3.co.tt

In the still┬şness of a Sat┬şur┬şday morn┬şing, a large ship docked on the wa┬şter┬şfront near the Hy┬şatt Re┬şgency in Port-of-Spain. There are many ships that dock on those wa┬şters week┬şly, but this ves┬şsel presents a glimpse in┬şto an in┬şdus┬ştry long┬şing to be dis┬şcov┬şered in this coun┬ştry.

The Fortes┬şcue Green Pi┬şo┬şneer has slow┬şly made its way from Belem, Brazil (where the UN Cli┬şmate Change Con┬şfer┬şence took place last No┬şvem┬şber) to the Port of Port-of-Spain. It will spend a week in T&T wa┬şters. How┬şev┬şer, the orig┬şi┬şnal sched┬şule was a mere pit stop for fu┬şel be┬şfore sail┬şing high┬şer up the Caribbean chain. That changed when in┬şdus┬ştry lead┬şers learned of the ves┬şselÔÇÖs unique┬şness.

The Green Pi┬şo┬şneer is the worldÔÇÖs first am┬şmo┬şnia-pow┬şered ves┬şsel, de┬şsigned to test and demon┬şstrate the vi┬şa┬şbil┬şi┬şty of am┬şmo┬şnia as a ze┬şro-emis┬şsion fu┬şel in the ship┬şping in┬şdus┬ştry. It was de┬şvel┬şoped and de┬şsigned by the Aus┬ştralian min┬şing com┬şpa┬şny, Fortes┬şcue. In 2025, it was list┬şed by Time Mag┬şa┬şzine as one of the best in┬şven┬ştions of the year: ÔÇťa sea┬şwor┬şthy so┬şlu┬ştion to ship┬şping pol┬şlu┬ştion.ÔÇŁ

The word am┬şmo┬şnia lit a bulb in the heads of Trinida┬şdi┬şans who wel┬şcomed the ves┬şsel.

This coun┬ştry is a glob┬şal leader in am┬şmo┬şnia pro┬şduc┬ştion and ex┬şports, boast┬şing eleven world-scale am┬şmo┬şnia plants with a to┬ştal an┬şnu┬şal ca┬şpac┬şi┬şty of ap┬şprox┬şi┬şmate┬şly 5.2 to 5.67 mil┬şlion met┬şric tonnes. The na┬ştion ranks as the worldÔÇÖs largest net ex┬şporter of am┬şmo┬şnia, fre┬şquent┬şly con┬ştribut┬şing 15 to 20 per cent of the to┬ştal glob┬şal┬şly trans┬şport┬şed am┬şmo┬şnia. If the mar┬şitime in┬şdus┬ştry de┬şcides to go in the di┬şrec┬ştion of am┬şmo┬şnia-fu┬şelled ves┬şsels, T&T is in a po┬şsi┬ştion ge┬şo┬şgraph┬şi┬şcal┬şly and lo┬şgis┬şti┬şcal┬şly to be a gas sta┬ştion for ships in this re┬şgion.

Philip Julien, chair┬şman of Ke┬şnes┬şjay Green Lim┬şit┬şed, who has led the coun┬ştryÔÇÖs thrust in┬şto low-car┬şbon and green hy┬şdro┬şgen as an ad┬şdi┬ştion┬şal feed┬şstock for the petro┬şchem┬şi┬şcal sec┬ştor, told the Sun┬şday Guardian, ÔÇťThis re┬şal┬şly rep┬şre┬şsents a strate┬şgic cross┬şroads that weÔÇÖre at.ÔÇŁ

He hailed these al┬şter┬şna┬ştive ma┬şrine fu┬şels as ÔÇťa new and emerg┬şing mar┬şketÔÇŁ that T&T has the po┬şten┬ştial to cre┬şate an in┬şdus┬ştry out of. This in┬şcludes it be┬şcom┬şing a re┬şgion┬şal hub for both tra┬şdi┬ştion┬şal and a va┬şri┬şety of low┬şer-car┬şbon ma┬şrine fu┬şels, with one ex┬şam┬şple be┬şing the pro┬şduc┬ştion, stor┬şage, bunker┬şing, and ex┬şport of am┬şmo┬şnia.

Head of Ma┬şrine Sys┬ştems and Green Ship┬şping at Fortes┬şcue, An┬şdrew Hoare, agreed with JulienÔÇÖs as┬şsess┬şment. In an in┬şter┬şview with the Sun┬şday Guardian, he said, ÔÇťI think thereÔÇÖs a very, very sig┬şnif┬şi┬şcant com┬şmer┬şcial im┬şpor┬ştance here. We can go to a beau┬şti┬şful, love┬şly Caribbean is┬şland, but theyÔÇÖre not go┬şing to nec┬şes┬şsar┬şi┬şly see the rel┬şe┬şvance as much as Trinidad and To┬şba┬şgo have, be┬şcause youÔÇÖve got one of the most im┬şpor┬ştant el┬şe┬şments. YouÔÇÖve got an in┬şfra┬şstruc┬şture that ex┬şists to┬şday. ItÔÇÖs been around for quite some time... ItÔÇÖs al┬şways been a key trans-ship┬şment hub. ItÔÇÖs been a key oil-fu┬şelling hub. I think itÔÇÖs a great op┬şpor┬ştu┬şni┬şty.ÔÇŁ

This week, stu┬şdents from var┬şi┬şous uni┬şver┬şsi┬şties will have a tour of the ship to learn about its key el┬şe┬şments. Hoare is look┬şing for┬şward to this week in Trinidad. He added, ÔÇťI think thatÔÇÖs go┬şing to be one of, hope┬şful┬şly, the most warm┬şing el┬şe┬şments of the run to Trinidad and To┬şba┬şgo. It is to out┬şreach with stake┬şhold┬şers there. IÔÇÖm sure thereÔÇÖs go┬şing to be high┬şer-lev┬şel, maybe gov┬şern┬şment-po┬şlit┬şi┬şcal types there as well, or the in┬şdus┬ştry types, but I think prob┬şa┬şbly the most warm┬şing will be the fact that weÔÇÖre not just steer┬şing the boats at the top. We are try┬şing to cre┬şate a mar┬şket, change a mar┬şket, and I think in the case of T&T, weÔÇÖre try┬şing to en┬şsure that you re┬ştain your po┬şsi┬ştion in that mar┬şket.ÔÇŁ

They see it as an op┬şpor┬ştu┬şni┬şty for them to show┬şcase the work theyÔÇÖre do┬şing in am┬şmo┬şnia-pow┬şered ships. The Green Pi┬şo┬şneer re┬şmains the on┬şly ship that has ac┬ştu┬şal┬şly proven in┬şter┬şna┬ştion┬şal move┬şment us┬şing am┬şmo┬şnia as a fu┬şel. How┬şev┬şer, Hoare said there are small pi┬şlots hap┬şpen┬şing in the ports, and their first ships donÔÇÖt come out un┬ştil the end of the year.

ÔÇťI think the ex┬şcit┬şing part of it is that it shows us a tan┬şgi┬şble ex┬şam┬şple of an al┬şter┬şna┬ştive and emerg┬şing fu┬şel that the world is look┬şing at for the mar┬şitime in┬şdus┬ştry,ÔÇŁ Julien said.

Pro┬şman is one of the mul┬şti-na┬ştion┬şal com┬şpa┬şnies in T&T pro┬şduc┬şing am┬şmo┬şnia. In a con┬şver┬şsa┬ştion with the Sun┬şday Busi┬şness Guardian last week, its Deputy Man┬şag┬şing Di┬şrec┬ştor, Giselle Thomp┬şson, said her com┬şpa┬şny has been us┬şing methanol as a fu┬şel for their mar┬şitime ser┬şvices. How┬şev┬şer, she added, ÔÇťThere is like┬şly room in the mar┬şket in terms of the mar┬şitime sec┬ştor for any fu┬şel that is low┬şer in car┬şbon in┬şten┬şsi┬şty, as the sec┬ştor has to move to some of those cli┬şmate goals and the tar┬şgets put out by the In┬şter┬şna┬ştion┬şal Mar┬şitime Or┬şgan┬şi┬şsa┬ştion (IMO). IÔÇÖm sure there is room in the mar┬şket for am┬şmo┬şnia-pow┬şered ships, LNG-pow┬şered ships, and methanol-pow┬şered ships.ÔÇŁ

Hoare and his team will em┬şbark on a Caribbean tour af┬şter their T&T vis┬şit. When asked why this re┬şgion was so im┬şpor┬ştant to their en┬şdeav┬şours, he an┬şswered, ÔÇťOur group res┬şonates close┬şly with the Caribbean states be┬şcause weÔÇÖre very close to the South Pa┬şcif┬şic is┬şland states of Aus┬ştralia. There are cer┬ştain sim┬şi┬şlar┬şi┬şties. WeÔÇÖve all seen the ter┬şri┬şble chal┬şlenges in Ja┬şmaica last year with poor weath┬şer, which we could prob┬şa┬şbly say may be cli┬şmate-re┬şlat┬şed. Like┬şwise, we see first┬şhand in the South Pa┬şcif┬şic is┬şlands the im┬şpact of ris┬şing sea lev┬şels there, with com┬şmu┬şni┬şties al┬şready be┬şing dis┬şplaced. Al┬şthough weÔÇÖre more aligned and cog┬şnizant of the South Pa┬şcif┬şic, I think by be┬şing in the Caribbean, it gives us a chance to lis┬şten and to show the ship be┬şyond a Pow┬şer┬şPoint pre┬şsen┬şta┬ştion.ÔÇŁ

Julien be┬şlieves the Gov┬şern┬şmentÔÇÖs Blue┬şprint Plan aligns with the po┬şten┬ştial of this mar┬şitime in┬şdus┬ştry change. How┬şev┬şer, when ques┬ştioned how far in┬şto the fu┬şture such a de┬şvel┬şop┬şment could be┬şcome a re┬şal┬şi┬şty, he stat┬şed, ÔÇťI think the hori┬şzon match┬şes the de┬şvel┬şop┬şment time. ThatÔÇÖs the best way to put it, so this isnÔÇÖt so far off that we can┬şnot start with the de┬şvel┬şop┬şment work now in or┬şder to de┬şvel┬şop those things that would make Trinidad a com┬şmer┬şcial┬şly vi┬şable and at┬ştrac┬ştive port of choice.ÔÇŁ

It comes as the In┬şter┬şna┬ştion┬şal Mar┬şitime Or┬şgan┬şi┬şsa┬ştion ac┬ştive┬şly pur┬şsues a fu┬şture for the ship┬şping in┬şdus┬ştry void of car┬şbon-emit┬şting fu┬şels. Last Oc┬şto┬şber, it was forced to walk back on a de┬şci┬şsion to ini┬şti┬şate the worldÔÇÖs first glob┬şal car┬şbon pric┬şing sys┬ştem on any in┬şter┬şna┬ştion┬şal pol┬şluter - glob┬şal ship┬şping. It was seen as a ma┬şjor set┬şback in meet┬şing the mar┬şitime in┬şdus┬ştryÔÇÖs de┬şcar┬şbon┬şi┬şsa┬ştion tar┬şgets. The Trump ad┬şmin┬şis┬ştra┬ştion in the Unit┬şed States was a staunch crit┬şic of the move.

How┬şev┬şer, while the bat┬ştle for a net-ze┬şro fu┬şture rages geopo┬şlit┬şi┬şcal┬şly, an op┬şpor┬ştu┬şni┬şty seems to have sailed on┬şto T&TÔÇÖs shores. The chal┬şlenge for every gov┬şern┬şment has been to see (and plan) for down the road as op┬şposed to ex┬ştract┬şing the fi┬şnite joys of the nat┬şur┬şal gas rich┬şes now. Julien, who has cham┬şpi┬şoned low-car┬şbon and green hy┬şdro┬şgen de┬şvel┬şop┬şment in this coun┬ştry, now sees an ad┬şdi┬ştion┬şal op┬şpor┬ştu┬şni┬şty for the coun┬ştryÔÇÖs eco┬şnom┬şic ex┬şpan┬şsion.

text:
Maritime Decarbonization Review 2025: IMO, EU ETS, and FuelEU Challenges

According to a publication from Stephenson Harwood, 2025 was a year in which maritime decarbonisation made headlines far beyond the maritime press. Provisional approval of the IMO Net Zero Framework in April and the postponement of the vote on its formal adoption in October attracted global attention.

The industry also grappled with the first verification period of the EU Emissions Trading Scheme (EU ETS) and the first reporting period of FuelEU Maritime. The proposed extension of the UK Emissions Trading Scheme took shape through final policy decisions, and other national regulators advanced plans for national emissions regulation.

Commercially, the drive for lower GHG intensity fuels brought the industry into contact with biofuel and alternative fuels markets, and a new compliance market emerged connected to FuelEU pooling. Contractual innovation was essential throughout the year to define responsibilities and liabilities amid regulatory, commercial, and technological change.

Key Questions for Owners and Charterers

The source text outlined five key questions for the industry. The first addressed whether emissions from a vessel that left the European Economic Area (EEA) before 1 January 2024 but was still on its departure voyage on that date are within the EU ETS. Different interpretations could leave the "shipping company" short of required allowances.

The second question considered continuing obligations under the EU MRV and EU ETS when selling a vessel that has been trading in Europe, noting penalties for non-compliance include publication of names, financial penalties, and potential vessel expulsion.

The third question urged parties to check charterparty EU ETS provisions, as shipping companies rely on the charterparty chain to obtain EU Allowances (EUAs) required for surrender by 30 September 2025. Gaps in supply could force last-minute purchases at market highs.

The fourth question examined the implications of using biofuels to reduce GHG intensity for charterparties and fuel supply contracts. It noted that typical charterparty terms do not adequately address biofuel handling, consumption, or valuation, and that fuel must meet specific sustainability criteria for regulatory benefits.

The fifth question explored considerations for FuelEU pooling and compensation for compliance surplus in time charterparties. It highlighted that FuelEU regulations leave most pooling details to private contract, making charterparty terms critical for determining which party benefits from a surplus and how it is valued.

title: NGOs Welcome Guidance on IMO Plans for Reducing Underwater Noise
description:
The Clean Arctic Alliance, made up of 24 not-for-profit organisations, is calling on member states to ensure the IMO continues to push for progress and action in reducing underwater noise from shipping, especially in Arctic waters where the levels of underwater noise are anticipated to nearly quadruple by 2030.

WWF invites mariners to use the maps in voyage planning, and urges member states and the IMO to implement location-specific rules and recommendations to reduce impacts of underwater noise in migratory corridors.
These developments increase the risks of air, water, and underwater noise pollution.
Many regions, such as in the Arctic and the Gulf of California in Mexico, share similar potential impacts from underwater noise, and both have seen low shipping traffic in the past.
text:
[By Clean Arctic Alliance]

As a meeting of the International Maritime OrganizationÔÇÖs (IMO) Sub-Committee on Ship Design and Construction (SDC 12) closes today, the Clean Arctic Alliance welcomed the clear guidance on how the IMO must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts, and the recommendation of a two year extension of the three-year ÔÇťexperience building phaseÔÇŁ (EBP).

During SDC 12, Member States finalized a list of key technical aspects for reducing underwater noise from shipping, which will inform future policy considerations by the Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC) during the proposed two-year extension of the Experience Building Phase, and will be initially considered by MEPC 84 in April.

The Clean Arctic Alliance, made up of 24 not-for-profit organisations, is calling on member states to ensure the IMO continues to push for progress and action in reducing underwater noise from shipping, especially in Arctic waters where the levels of underwater noise are anticipated to nearly quadruple by 2030.

ÔÇťDuring this weekÔÇÖs IMO meeting, member states agreed on clear guidance on how we must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts during the proposed extension of the experience building phase. At the two forthcoming MEPC meetings this year, we hope to pursue policy that will move beyond voluntary guidanceÔÇŁ, said Sarah Bobbe, Senior Manager, Arctic Program at Ocean Conservancy, a member of both the Clean Arctic Alliance and the Clean Shipping Coalition. ÔÇťSo far, this work has not kept pace relative to the growing threats faced by regions experiencing increased shipping traffic, such as the Arctic, underscoring the need for parallel policy advancement through the IMOÔÇÖs Marine Environmental Protection Committee in the coming months.ÔÇŁ

For many marine organisms sound is the most important means of communication. Under water vision is restricted and without good hearing ability, elementary functions such as navigating, finding prey and partners can be hampered. This is of particular importance for marine mammals, and also for fish and even invertebrates.

ÔÇťMuch of the Arctic has been free of anthropogenic (human-sourced) sound for a long time, but with increased human activity, Arctic waters are becoming more noisy every yearÔÇŁ, said Bobbe. ÔÇťAn important source of noise is shipping, specifically from propellers and engines. Since shipping has increased substantially during recent decades, underwater noise is a growing problem, contributing to serious impact on the Arctic ecosystem.ÔÇŁ

Key IMO Papers:

A paper submitted to SDC 12 by WWF and presented during a lunchtime side event, SDC 12-INF.17 ÔÇô Spatio-temporal information on Arctic whale migration routes for consideration in voyage planning and other measures to reduce the impacts of underwater radiated noise on marine life provides new maps that show, for the first time, the migratory corridors of Arctic whales across the Arctic Ocean.

Marine mammal migratory corridors are specified in both the URN Guidelines and the Polar Code as areas of special concern because they contain high concentrations of animals sensitive to impacts of shipping. The maps provide mariners with new information on where they need to take measures in migratory months, which in the Arctic, are in spring and autumn. Operational measures, such as rerouting and restricting speed, can reduce impacts on migrating whales, and these measures go hand in hand with technical measures to quieten ships through new builds and modifications. WWF invites mariners to use the maps in voyage planning, and urges member states and the IMO to implement location-specific rules and recommendations to reduce impacts of underwater noise in migratory corridors.

ÔÇťBy putting migratory corridors on the map for these three whale species that we know to be especially sensitive to underwater noise, we hope to support the shipping community to take necessary measures to safeguard themÔÇŁ, said Melanie Lancaster, Senior Specialist, Arctic species conservation for WWFÔÇÖs Global Arctic Programme and member of the Clean Arctic Alliance. ÔÇťFrom the maps, we see that whales migrate impressive distances between their summer and winter habitats, some swimming thousands of kilometres across national borders and even into the high seas of the Central Arctic Ocean. It will take international cooperation among states, ship operators, shipping companies and IMO to ensure their safe and quiet passage.ÔÇŁ

The WWF paper outlines how IMO Member States should consider adopting shipping measures in the Arctic that build on the practice within IMO to safeguard whales from the adverse impacts of shipping.

Mandatory ship reporting systems and recommendatory seasonal areas to be avoided have been adopted for North Atlantic right whales along the United States east coast and recommendatory speed restrictions have been adopted as an Associated Protective Measure (APM) for whales in the North-Western Mediterranean Sea Particularly Sensitive Sea Area (PSSA). The paper provides the necessary information for similar preventative measures to be adopted to safeguard whales in the Arctic.

A paper submitted to SDC 12 by the Clean Shipping Coalition - SDC 12/8/3 and sent onto MEPC 84 for consideration, shares the findings of a study on the potential underwater radiated noise (URN) impacts of LNG development on marine mammals in the Gulf of California. The paper examines the potential effects of URN from LNG tanker traffic on migratory and resident whales, as well as related impacts on air quality and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.

The proposed LNG terminals and the resulting increase in LNG tanker traffic are expected to pose serious threats to the Gulf of California's unique biodiversity and conservation areas, including those designated as a UNESCO "World Heritage Site" and "Important Marine Mammal Areas". These developments increase the risks of air, water, and underwater noise pollution. Given the Gulf of California's critical ecological roleÔÇöparticularly as a habitat for resident and migratory marine megafaunaÔÇöa precautionary approach to industrial development is essential. It is increasingly evident that the region's ecological integrity is incompatible with the scale and nature of heavy marine traffic associated with proposed LNG facilities.

Many regions, such as in the Arctic and the Gulf of California in Mexico, share similar potential impacts from underwater noise, and both have seen low shipping traffic in the past. Shipping traffic has been increasing in the last decade in the Arctic, while in the Gulf of California the developing LNG infrastructure will bring unprecedented vessel traffic to a marine area with enormous ecological value, in particular for marine mammals.

ÔÇťOur recommendations to SDC 12, and to the forthcoming discussions at MEPC 84, for the Gulf of California are equally important for the Arctic as both regions could see devastating impacts from future increased shipping, especially from LNG tankers, for marine wildlife particularly sensitive to noise, like marine mammalsÔÇŁ, said Andrew Dumbrille, Co-Founder and Director of Equal Routes - a member of both the Clean Arctic Alliance and the Clean Shipping Coalition. ÔÇťThe recommendations include: acknowledging the irreversible impacts of underwater noise caused by LNG tanker traffic on marine mammals' behaviours and habitat; promoting the development of ship design that takes into account the serious damage underwater noise causes to the marine habitat; and opposing LNG traffic in critical ecological marine areas because of, among others, the underwater noise pollution it causes, affecting marine mammals and other marine species. Both the Gulf of California and the Arctic are marine areas of incredible ecological, social and biodiversity importance for the planet, the impacts of increased noise would change the quality of the marine habitats irrevocably.ÔÇŁ

description:
Researchers have conducted an analysis of high-pressure dual-injection (HPDI) systems, focusing on the use of methanol as a fuel source for maritime applications.
The study emphasizes the significance of understanding nozzle behavior and fluid dynamics within these systems to improve efficiency, reduce emissions, and address growing energy demands in the shipping industry.

This approach allowed researchers to analyze critical aspects such as spray formation, fuel atomization, and combustion processes.
Findings from the study aim to contribute to advancements in fuel injection technology by providing insights into optimizing injector design and performance for cleaner and more efficient maritime operations.

title: Danish study shows biofuel cuts harmful ship emissions up to 81% - BioEnergy Times
description:
A new Danish study based on real-life ship operations has found that using biofuel can significantly reduce harmful emissions from the shipping sector.
They noted that biodiesel not only lowers overall carbon emissions but also sharply reduces black carbon, which plays a key role in the shipping industryÔÇÖs climate impact.
Officials from Uni-Tankers said the real-world findings offer valuable insight into both soot and carbon emissions linked to biodiesel use.
Researchers said further analysis is ongoing to identify practical ways to reduce black carbon and other emissions from ships.
The document is expected to support international efforts to curb black carbon emissions, especially in sensitive regions such as the Arctic.
text:
A new Danish study based on real-life ship operations has found that using biofuel can significantly reduce harmful emissions from the shipping sector. The findings have been submitted to the International Maritime Organization (IMO) for review, reports Biodiesel Magazine.

The study highlights major reductions in black carbon, a form of soot released by ships that worsens air pollution, affects human health and accelerates global warming, particularly in the Arctic. Experts involved in the CLEANSHIP project reported that ships running on biodiesel, specifically B100 FAME, recorded up to an 81 per cent drop in black carbon emissions compared with those using conventional marine fuel.

The data was collected from the Uni-Tankers vessel Falstria Swan during normal sailing operations. An advanced monitoring device, developed by Green Instruments in partnership with the Danish Technological Institute, enabled continuous and real-time tracking of emissions while the ship was in regular service.

Researchers said the results were stronger than expected, especially at lower engine power levels. They noted that biodiesel not only lowers overall carbon emissions but also sharply reduces black carbon, which plays a key role in the shipping industryÔÇÖs climate impact. The new monitoring technology also provided more detailed emission data than previously possible.

Officials from Uni-Tankers said the real-world findings offer valuable insight into both soot and carbon emissions linked to biodiesel use. They added that this information will help the company meet its emission reduction goals and support broader efforts to reduce pollution across the shipping sector.

Despite the positive results, the study noted that current global biofuel production is not sufficient to meet the full energy needs of the global shipping fleet. Researchers said further analysis is ongoing to identify practical ways to reduce black carbon and other emissions from ships.

The findings have been compiled into a white paper and submitted to the IMO. The document is expected to support international efforts to curb black carbon emissions, especially in sensitive regions such as the Arctic. It is scheduled to be reviewed at the IMOÔÇÖs Sub-Committee on Pollution Prevention and Response meeting in February 2026.

text:
CF Industries, Trafigura, TFG Marine Sign MOU for Low-Carbon Ammonia Marine Fuel

CF Industries Holdings Inc., Trafigura, and TFG Marine announced the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding to facilitate the adoption of low-carbon ammonia as a marine fuel, according to Hydrogen Central. The agreement establishes a framework for the parties to work together on advancing low-carbon ammonia as a marine fuel, supporting the global shipping industry's emissions-reduction efforts.

Under the MOU, the companies will collaborate on key initiatives including market development, stakeholder engagement, and bunkering logistics planning. The collaboration will initially focus on the U.S. Gulf Coast and Northwest Europe.

CF Industries will leverage its low-carbon ammonia production and export capabilities at its Donaldsonville, Louisiana, complex. Trafigura will contribute its expertise in commodity logistics and market development. TFG Marine, a joint venture between Trafigura, Frontline and Golden Ocean Group, will leverage its global marine fuel supply network and bunkering capabilities to support last-mile delivery solutions, coordinate ammonia bunkering demand, and transport ammonia to bunkering hubs globally.

Bert Frost, Executive Vice President and Chief Commercial Officer at CF Industries, said: "We are pleased to collaborate with industry leaders such as Trafigura and TFG Marine to establish the supply chain necessary to meet the expected transition of the marine shipping industry to low-carbon ammonia as a fuel."

Patricio Norris, Global Head of Ammonia and LPG at Trafigura, said: "This agreement brings together the critical components needed to advance low-carbon ammonia as a viable marine fuel. Through this collaboration with CF Industries and TFG Marine, we are combining world-class production capabilities with our global logistics and bunkering network to support the maritime industry's transition to cleaner fuels."

Kenneth Dam, Executive Director of TFG Marine, said: "Our global bunkering infrastructure and operational expertise position us to play a key role in delivering low-carbon ammonia fuel solutions to the shipping industry. This collaboration with CF Industries and Trafigura enables us to develop the supply chain and logistics capabilities needed to make low-carbon ammonia bunkering a commercial reality for vessel operators worldwide."

text:
The head of the United Nations body governing the global shipping industry has said that greenhouse gases from the global shipping industry will fall, whether or not the sectorÔÇÖs ÔÇťNet Zero FrameworkÔÇŁ to cut emissions is adopted in October.

Arsenio Dominguez, secretary-general of the International Maritime Organization, told a new yearÔÇÖs press conference in London on Friday that, even if governments donÔÇÖt sign up to the framework later this year as planned, the clean-up of the industry responsible for 3% of global emissions will continue.

ÔÇťI reiterate my call to industry that the decarbonisation has started. ThereÔÇÖs lots of research and development that is ongoing. ThereÔÇÖs new plans on alternative fuels like methanol and ammonia that continue to evolve,ÔÇŁ he told journalists.

He said he has not heard any government dispute a set of decarbonisation goals agreed in 2023. These include targets to reduce emissions 20-30% on 2008 levels by 2030 and then to reach net zero emissions ÔÇťby or around, i.e. close to 2050ÔÇŁ.

Dominguez said the 2030 emissions reduction target could be reached, although a goal for shipping to use at least 5% clean fuels by 2030 would be difficult to meet because their cost will remain high until at least the 2030s. The goals agreed in 2023 also included cutting emissions by 70-80% by 2040.

In October 2025, a decision on a proposed framework of practical measures to achieve the goals, which aims to incentivise shipowners to go green by taxing polluting ships and subsidising cleaner ones, was postponed by a year after a narrow vote by governments.

Ahead of that vote, the US threatened governments and their officials with sanctions, tariffs and visa restrictions ÔÇô and President Donald Trump called the framework a ÔÇťGreen New Scam Tax on ShippingÔÇŁ.

Dominguez said at FridayÔÇÖs press conference that he had not received any official complaints about the USÔÇÖs behaviour at last OctoberÔÇÖs meeting but ÔÇô without naming names ÔÇô he called on nations to be ÔÇťmore respectfulÔÇŁ at the IMO. He added that he did not think the US would leave the IMO, saying Washington had engaged constructively on the organisationÔÇÖs budget and plans.

EU urged to clarify ETS position

The European Union ÔÇô along with Brazil and Pacific island nations ÔÇô pushed hard for the framework to be adopted in October. Some developing countries were concerned that the EU would retain its charges for polluting ships under its emissions trading scheme (ETS), even if the Net Zero Framework was passed, leading to ships travelling to and from the EU being charged twice.

This was an uncertainty that the US and Saudi Arabia exploited at the meeting to try and win over wavering developing countries. Most African, Asian and Caribbean nations voted for a delay.

On Friday, Dominguez called on the EU ÔÇťto clarify their position on the review of the ETS, in order that as we move forward, we actually donÔÇÖt have two systems that are going to be basically looking for the same the same goal, the same objective.ÔÇŁ

He said he would continue to speak to EU member states, ÔÇťto maintain the conversations in here, rather than move forward into fragmentation, because that will have a very detrimental effect in shippingÔÇŁ. ÔÇťThat would really create difficulties for operators, that would increase the cost, and everybodyÔÇÖs going to suffer from it,ÔÇŁ he added.

The IMOÔÇÖs marine environment protection committee, in which governments discuss climate strategy, will meet in April although the Net Zero Framework is not scheduled to be officially discussed until October.

name: International Maritime Organization
title: Efforts to Cut Down Ship Emissions Is Now Dramatically Harming Ocean Health, Scientists Discover
description:
The study claims that it has enhanced a coral bleaching event on the Great Barrier Reef.
This elevation led to 10% more heat stress on the corals of the Great Barrier Reef during the mass bleaching event.
This decision proved to be fruitful, as it reduced harmful sulfur emissions and improved air quality.
Despite the intensification of coral reef bleaching, experts do not think that the regulations should be called back.
To prevent coral reef bleaching, greenhouse gas emissions need to be reduced, which lowers global warming, which further triggers the bleaching.
text:
Environmentalists worldwide were elated when the decision to reduce sulfur from shipping fuels was announced. However, the call has had some worrisome aftereffects, as published in the journal Communications Earth and Environment. The study claims that it has enhanced a coral bleaching event on the Great Barrier Reef. This event took place back in the early months of 2022, when the reduction of sulfur emissions from the ship allowed more than typical amounts of sunlight to reach the reef. This elevation led to 10% more heat stress on the corals of the Great Barrier Reef during the mass bleaching event. It was the first bleaching event to unfold in a La Ni├▒a year.

The limitation was put into effect by the International Maritime OrganizationÔÇÖs (IMO) 2020 regulation. As per this regulation, the sulfur content in the marine fuels was reduced from 3.5% to 0.5%. This decision proved to be fruitful, as it reduced harmful sulfur emissions and improved air quality. However, it also hampered a cooling effect in the atmosphere and particularly AustraliaÔÇÖs Great Barrier Reef. In this cooling phenomenon, sulfur becomes a sort of layer that prevents some amount of sunlight from reaching the surface. Reduction of sulfur from the fuel led to less of this element in the atmosphere. This weakened the layer, causing more sunlight than before to reach the surface.

To make the assertions, researchers considered the ship data of the region from 18 to 28 February 2022. During this time, 5,000 ships supposedly moved in the Great Barrier Reef. The heat was progressively building up on the reef at that time. After gathering the data, they created computer models to figure out the amount of sulfur emission in the setup with the regulation, and what it would have been without it. The models essentially try to shed light on how sulfur particles emitted from the vessels drifted over the reef, and the cooling it facilitated.

The team also evaluated the weather conditions at the site to understand how sulfur moved in the air. Past observations had revealed that prevailing winds in the region blow the sulfur from above the reef. The results indicated that 5% to 10% more solar energy was hitting the reefÔÇÖs waters with regulations than it would have without limitations. Researchers believe that the increased amount of sunlight, due to reduced sulfur emissions from the vessels, intensified the 2022 coral bleaching event on the Great Barrier Reef. The intensification is dangerous as it further hampers the marine ecosystem.

This is not an isolated event. The cooling effect occurs worldwide, and hence the removal of sulfur also increases temperatures across the globe, due to an enhanced amount of sunlight. Professor Steven Sherwood, a climate scientist at the University of New South Wales, not involved in the research, shared that the removal of sulfur aerosols from the atmosphere due to reduced emissions elevated the global temperatures by 32F. ÔÇťThis new study points out that while the overall effect on climate is small, the local effect on ocean temperatures in the tropics can be larger. This is because the air pollution absorbs some sunlight in addition to reflection to space,ÔÇŁ Sherwood said, per The Guardian.

ÔÇťThus, they find a bigger effect, maybe up to 0.15 ┬░C (32.27 ┬░F), which is enough to increase the heat stress on the corals as reported. This is an important result since it shows the impact on corals might be nontrivial even though the overall global warming impact is pretty small,ÔÇŁ Sherwood added. Despite the intensification of coral reef bleaching, experts do not think that the regulations should be called back. Reduction of sulfur pollution is essential for safeguarding human health. To prevent coral reef bleaching, greenhouse gas emissions need to be reduced, which lowers global warming, which further triggers the bleaching.

name: Biofuels & Shipping
title: Biodiesel reduces ship's black carbon emissions by up to 81%
description:
Now, experts from the CLEANSHIP project document that using biodiesel, specifically B100 FAME, during normal operation can reduce Black Carbon emissions by up to 81 percent compared to traditional marine gas oil (MGO).
This demonstrates that biodiesel can reduce not only net COÔéé, but also Black Carbon, which is highly significant for the shipping sectorÔÇÖs total climate footprint.
Although biodiesel demonstrates a significant reduction in Black Carbon, current global production of biofuel can only cover a small share of the global fleetÔÇÖs total energy demand.
Therefore, the Cleanship project continues to analyse data and work towards recommendations on how Black Carbon and other emissions can be effectively reduced in shipping.
This documentation will contribute to the international efforts to reduce Black Carbon emissions ÔÇô especially in Arctic regions, where soot particles have a particularly significant climate impact on ice and the environment.
text:
New Danish data, collected during real-world sailing, documents significant climate benefits from biofuel for the shipping industry. The findings have been submitted to the International Maritime Organization (IMO).

Soot particles ÔÇô also known as black carbon ÔÇô are among the most harmful emissions from shipping, both for the climate, where they contribute to global warming and melting of Arctic ice, and for public health through poor air quality. Now, experts from the CLEANSHIP project document that using biodiesel, specifically B100 FAME, during normal operation can reduce Black Carbon emissions by up to 81 percent compared to traditional marine gas oil (MGO).

The measurements were carried out on the Uni-Tankers vessel Falstria Swan, where an advanced online Black Carbon sensor from Green Instruments, developed in collaboration with Danish Technological Institute, made it possible to track emissions in detail and in real time during the vesselÔÇÖs normal operation.

ÔÇťWe expected that biofuel would lead to cleaner combustion, but the magnitude of the reduction ÔÇô especially at lower engine loads ÔÇô is a very positive surprise. This demonstrates that biodiesel can reduce not only net COÔéé, but also Black Carbon, which is highly significant for the shipping sectorÔÇÖs total climate footprint. At the same time, the measurement technology allows us to monitor emissions with much greater detail than ever before,ÔÇŁ says Simon Bastrup Spangenberg, consultant and measurement specialist in alternative fuels and emission reduction at Danish Technological Institute.

For Uni-Tankers, the measurements translate directly into new, practical knowledge:

ÔÇťThe latest real-world sailing data on biodiesel (B100) give us a clear picture of both the black carbon footprint and actual COÔéé emissions. This insight is crucial for achieving our own reduction targets, while also contributing to better, data-driven recommendations for reducing emissions across the shipping industry,ÔÇŁ says Troels Reppien, Technical Director at Uni-Tankers.

Although biodiesel demonstrates a significant reduction in Black Carbon, current global production of biofuel can only cover a small share of the global fleetÔÇÖs total energy demand. Therefore, the Cleanship project continues to analyse data and work towards recommendations on how Black Carbon and other emissions can be effectively reduced in shipping.

The results from the measurement campaign have now been submitted to the International Maritime Organization (IMO) in the form of a white paper. This documentation will contribute to the international efforts to reduce Black Carbon emissions ÔÇô especially in Arctic regions, where soot particles have a particularly significant climate impact on ice and the environment. The white paper is scheduled for consideration at IMOÔÇÖs 13th session of the Sub-Committee on Pollution Prevention and Response (PPR), which will take place from 9ÔÇô13 February 2026.

title: Interferry Calls for Freeze on ETS - Money-Tourism.gr
description:
According to Interferry, any increase in fares due to ETS costs risks pushing freight back onto roads, undermining EuropeÔÇÖs long-standing strategy of shifting transport from land to sea.
The association recalls that it accepted the inclusion of shipping in the ETS on the condition that revenues would be reinvested in decarbonising the sector and that road transport would soon follow.
However, the recent decision by the Council to continue exempting road transport has raised serious concerns, as end users of maritime transport ÔÇö including island communities ÔÇö are being asked to shoulder the cost unilaterally.
ÔÇťExcluding road transport creates an immediate and serious competitive disadvantage for ferries,ÔÇŁ said Interferry Director of Regulatory Affairs Johan Rous.
ÔÇťThe current approach neither strengthens competitiveness nor cohesion and limits the sectorÔÇÖs ability to invest in cleaner technologies,ÔÇŁ Rous said.
text:
Interferry, the global association representing the ferry industry, is calling for an immediate freeze on the further application of the European UnionÔÇÖs Emissions Trading System (ETS) to shipping, warning that the current framework creates serious competitive distortions and risks shifting passengers and freight back to EuropeÔÇÖs already congested road networks.

The organisation urges the EU to keep the obligation to surrender emission allowances at the 70% level applied in 2025 and to suspend the planned increase to 100% from 2026, until road transport is also included in the ETS and a clear framework is established for channeling revenues towards the decarbonisation of shipping.

ÔÇťThe EU must honour its promise of a level playing field and ensure that climate policy supports, rather than financially drains, the most progressive transport sector,ÔÇŁ said Interferry CEO Mike Corrigan, stressing that the measure should remain on hold until road transport is incorporated into the system.

The ferry sector plays a critical role in European transport, with more than 50% of the worldÔÇÖs RoRo and passenger vessel fleet operating in European waters, carrying around 400 million passengers and 200 million vehicles and freight units annually within the EU. According to Interferry, any increase in fares due to ETS costs risks pushing freight back onto roads, undermining EuropeÔÇÖs long-standing strategy of shifting transport from land to sea.

The association recalls that it accepted the inclusion of shipping in the ETS on the condition that revenues would be reinvested in decarbonising the sector and that road transport would soon follow. However, the recent decision by the Council to continue exempting road transport has raised serious concerns, as end users of maritime transport ÔÇö including island communities ÔÇö are being asked to shoulder the cost unilaterally.

ÔÇťExcluding road transport creates an immediate and serious competitive disadvantage for ferries,ÔÇŁ said Interferry Director of Regulatory Affairs Johan Rous. ÔÇťThe ETS, as applied today, encourages the transfer of passengers and freight back to roads, directly contradicting EuropeÔÇÖs long-standing policy of shifting transport from land to sea.ÔÇŁ

At the same time, the International Maritime OrganizationÔÇÖs decision in October 2025 to postpone the adoption of a global carbon pricing mechanism for at least 12 months leaves the sector without a clear international framework and without certainty over when ÔÇö or if ÔÇö a unified system will be implemented.

According to Interferry, the ETS currently imposes an annual burden of around ÔéČ1 billion on intra-European ferry services, at a time when the sector requires support for the production of e-fuels and major investments in port electrification to serve electric-powered vessels. Instead, the organisation says, most of the revenues are being channelled into national budgets of member states.

ÔÇťThe current approach neither strengthens competitiveness nor cohesion and limits the sectorÔÇÖs ability to invest in cleaner technologies,ÔÇŁ Rous said.

Interferry, which has more than 280 members in over 40 countries, is calling on European authorities to reassess the policy to ensure that the green transition in shipping is accompanied by equal competitive conditions and genuine reinvestment of resources in the sectorÔÇÖs decarbonisation.

name: IMO & Shipping
title: SDC 12: NGOs welcome clear guidance on IMO plans for reducing underwater noise from shipping
description:
The Clean Arctic Alliance, made up of 24 not-for-profit organizations, is calling on member states to ensure the IMO continues to push for progress and action in reducing underwater noise from shipping, especially in Arctic waters where the levels of underwater noise are anticipated to nearly quadruple by 2030.
ÔÇťDuring this weekÔÇÖs IMO meeting, member states agreed on clear guidance on how we must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts during the proposed extension of the experience building phase.
At the two forthcoming MEPC meetings this year, we hope to pursue policy that will move beyond voluntary guidanceÔÇŁ, said Sarah Bobbe, Senior Manager, Arctic Program at Ocean Conservancy, a member of both the Clean Arctic Alliance and the Clean Shipping Coalition.
These developments increase the risks of air, water, and underwater noise pollution.
Many regions, such as in the Arctic and the Gulf of California in Mexico, share similar potential impacts from underwater noise, and both have seen low shipping traffic in the past.
text:
As a meeting of the International Maritime OrganizationÔÇÖs (IMO) Sub-Committee on Ship Design and Construction (SDC 12) closes, the Clean Arctic Alliance welcomed the clear guidance on how the IMO must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts, and the recommendation of a two year extension of the three-year ÔÇťexperience building phaseÔÇŁ (EBP).

During SDC 12, Member States finalized a list of key technical aspects for reducing underwater noise from shipping, which will inform future policy considerations by the Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC) during the proposed two-year extension of the Experience Building Phase, and will be initially considered by MEPC 84 in April.

The Clean Arctic Alliance, made up of 24 not-for-profit organizations, is calling on member states to ensure the IMO continues to push for progress and action in reducing underwater noise from shipping, especially in Arctic waters where the levels of underwater noise are anticipated to nearly quadruple by 2030.

ÔÇťDuring this weekÔÇÖs IMO meeting, member states agreed on clear guidance on how we must reduce underwater noise pollution and its impacts during the proposed extension of the experience building phase. At the two forthcoming MEPC meetings this year, we hope to pursue policy that will move beyond voluntary guidanceÔÇŁ, said Sarah Bobbe, Senior Manager, Arctic Program at Ocean Conservancy, a member of both the Clean Arctic Alliance and the Clean Shipping Coalition. ÔÇťSo far, this work has not kept pace relative to the growing threats faced by regions experiencing increased shipping traffic, such as the Arctic, underscoring the need for parallel policy advancement through the IMOÔÇÖs Marine Environmental Protection Committee in the coming months.ÔÇŁ

For many marine organisms sound is the most important means of communication. Under water vision is restricted and without good hearing ability, elementary functions such as navigating, finding prey and partners can be hampered. This is of particular importance for marine mammals, and also for fish and even invertebrates.

ÔÇťMuch of the Arctic has been free of anthropogenic (human-sourced) sound for a long time, but with increased human activity, Arctic waters are becoming more noisy every yearÔÇŁ, said Bobbe. ÔÇťAn important source of noise is shipping, specifically from propellers and engines. Since shipping has increased substantially during recent decades, underwater noise is a growing problem, contributing to serious impact on the Arctic ecosystem.ÔÇŁ

A paper submitted to SDC 12 by WWF and presented during a lunchtime side event, SDC 12-INF.17 ÔÇô Spatio-temporal information on Arctic whale migration routes for consideration in voyage planning and other measures to reduce the impacts of underwater radiated noise on marine life provides new maps that show, for the first time, the migratory corridors of Arctic whales across the Arctic Ocean.

Marine mammal migratory corridors are specified in both the URN Guidelines and the Polar Code as areas of special concern because they contain high concentrations of animals sensitive to impacts of shipping. The maps provide mariners with new information on where they need to take measures in migratory months, which in the Arctic, are in spring and autumn. Operational measures, such as rerouting and restricting speed, can reduce impacts on migrating whales, and these measures go hand in hand with technical measures to quieten ships through new builds and modifications. WWF invites mariners to use the maps in voyage planning, and urges member states and the IMO to implement location-specific rules and recommendations to reduce impacts of underwater noise in migratory corridors.

ÔÇťBy putting migratory corridors on the map for these three whale species that we know to be especially sensitive to underwater noise, we hope to support the shipping community to take necessary measures to safeguard themÔÇŁ, said Melanie Lancaster, Senior Specialist, Arctic species conservation for WWFÔÇÖs Global Arctic Program and member of the Clean Arctic Alliance. ÔÇťFrom the maps, we see that whales migrate impressive distances between their summer and winter habitats, some swimming thousands of kilometers across national borders and even into the high seas of the Central Arctic Ocean. It will take international cooperation among states, ship operators, shipping companies and IMO to ensure their safe and quiet passage.ÔÇŁ

The WWF paper outlines how IMO Member States should consider adopting shipping measures in the Arctic that build on the practice within IMO to safeguard whales from the adverse impacts of shipping.

Mandatory ship reporting systems and recommendatory seasonal areas to be avoided have been adopted for North Atlantic right whales along the United States east coast and recommendatory speed restrictions have been adopted as an Associated Protective Measure (APM) for whales in the North-Western Mediterranean Sea Particularly Sensitive Sea Area (PSSA). The paper provides the necessary information for similar preventative measures to be adopted to safeguard whales in the Arctic.

A paper submitted to SDC 12 by the Clean Shipping Coalition - SDC 12/8/3 and sent onto MEPC 84 for consideration, shares the findings of a study on the potential underwater radiated noise (URN) impacts of LNG development on marine mammals in the Gulf of California. The paper examines the potential effects of URN from LNG tanker traffic on migratory and resident whales, as well as related impacts on air quality and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.

The proposed LNG terminals and the resulting increase in LNG tanker traffic are expected to pose serious threats to the Gulf of California's unique biodiversity and conservation areas, including those designated as a UNESCO "World Heritage Site" and "Important Marine Mammal Areas". These developments increase the risks of air, water, and underwater noise pollution. Given the Gulf of California's critical ecological roleÔÇöparticularly as a habitat for resident and migratory marine megafaunaÔÇöa precautionary approach to industrial development is essential. It is increasingly evident that the region's ecological integrity is incompatible with the scale and nature of heavy marine traffic associated with proposed LNG facilities.

Many regions, such as in the Arctic and the Gulf of California in Mexico, share similar potential impacts from underwater noise, and both have seen low shipping traffic in the past. Shipping traffic has been increasing in the last decade in the Arctic, while in the Gulf of California the developing LNG infrastructure will bring unprecedented vessel traffic to a marine area with enormous ecological value, in particular for marine mammals.

ÔÇťOur recommendations to SDC 12, and to the forthcoming discussions at MEPC 84, for the Gulf of California are equally important for the Arctic as both regions could see devastating impacts from future increased shipping, especially from LNG tankers, for marine wildlife particularly sensitive to noise, like marine mammalsÔÇŁ, said Andrew Dumbrille, Co-Founder and Director of Equal Routes - a member of both the Clean Arctic Alliance and the Clean Shipping Coalition. ÔÇťThe recommendations include: acknowledging the irreversible impacts of underwater noise caused by LNG tanker traffic on marine mammals' behaviors and habitat; promoting the development of ship design that takes into account the serious damage underwater noise causes to the marine habitat; and opposing LNG traffic in critical ecological marine areas because of, among others, the underwater noise pollution it causes, affecting marine mammals and other marine species. Both the Gulf of California and the Arctic are marine areas of incredible ecological, social and biodiversity importance for the planet, the impacts of increased noise would change the quality of the marine habitats irrevocably.ÔÇŁ

text:
Dublin, Jan. 23, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- The "Green Methanol Ships Market - Global Industry Size, Share, Trends, Opportunity, and Forecast, 2021-2031" has been added to ResearchAndMarkets.com's offering.

The Global Green Methanol Ships Market is projected to expand from USD 5.21 Billion in 2025 to USD 12.67 Billion by 2031, registering a CAGR of 15.96%.

These vessels utilize methanol derived from renewable feedstocks, such as biomass or captured carbon dioxide mixed with green hydrogen, offering a sustainable substitute for traditional heavy fuel oil. The market is primarily driven by strict international decarbonization mandates, including the International Maritime Organization's emission reduction targets, alongside the shipping industry's voluntary pursuit of net-zero operations. According to the Methanol Institute, in 2025, the global fleet of methanol-fueled vessels, including those on order, exceeded 360 units, highlighting the sector's rapid shift toward alternative propulsion technologies to comply with tightening environmental regulations.

Despite this growth, the market faces significant hurdles regarding the scalability of fuel production and supply infrastructure. A major obstacle hindering broader expansion is the high production cost and limited availability of green methanol compared to fossil-based alternatives, which creates economic uncertainty for fleet operators attempting to secure long-term supply contracts.

Market Drivers

The surge in newbuild orders for methanol-powered vessels acts as a primary catalyst for market growth, creating a guaranteed demand baseline that forces stakeholders to accelerate the transition from fossil fuels. This momentum is particularly strong in the container segment, where major carriers are aggressively renewing fleets to hedge against future regulatory costs and secure early-mover advantages. According to DNV's 'Alternative Fuels Insights' from January 2025, methanol gained substantial traction with 166 orders in 2024, accounting for 32% of the alternative fuel orderbook. This robust contracting activity is validated by the operational reality of the technology; the Global Maritime Forum's 'From Pilots to Practice' report in August 2025 noted that the sector has moved beyond testing, with more than 60 methanol-capable vessels already in operation demonstrating technical viability.

Parallel to fleet expansion, increasing investments in green methanol production capacity are critical in resolving fuel availability bottlenecks. Energy developers are scaling up renewable fuel projects globally to meet the shipping industry's demand signals, ensuring long-term supply security and price stability. According to the Methanol Institute's November 2025 'Renewable Methanol' database update, the global project pipeline has expanded to track 252 renewable methanol projects with a total anticipated capacity of 45.1 million metric tons by 2030. This infrastructure expansion is vital for ensuring that the growing number of dual-fuel vessels can operate on green methanol rather than conventional fuels, thereby enabling the industry to meet its decarbonization targets.

Market Challenges

The limited scalability of green methanol production and associated high costs present a substantial barrier to market progression. While the maritime industry is seeing a surge in vessel orders, the supply chain for the necessary renewable fuel remains underdeveloped and financially restrictive. This discrepancy creates a volatile economic environment where fleet operators face elevated operational expenditures compared to those relying on fossil-based alternatives. Consequently, the uncertainty surrounding the consistent availability of green methanol prevents shipping companies from securing viable long-term supply contracts, introducing significant financial risk to their decarbonization strategies.

This supply-demand imbalance acts as a direct brake on broader commercial adoption, as the infrastructure required to produce and bunker green methanol globally is not expanding fast enough to match growing fleet capacity. According to the Methanol Institute, in 2024, the project pipeline for renewable methanol exceeded 21 million metric tons of potential annual capacity, yet the actual volume available for immediate maritime use remained critically low due to delayed final investment decisions. This gap between planned capacity and deliverable fuel highlights the logistical constraints that currently hinder the market's ability to support a full-scale transition.

Market Trends

The Rising Dominance of Asian Shipyards in Methanol Vessel Construction is reshaping the market, as regional builders leverage immense capacity to secure the majority of dual-fuel contracts. This trend is driven by their ability to offer competitive pricing and rapid delivery slots for complex vessels, effectively centralizing the global supply chain in Asia. According to Seatrade Maritime's January 2025 report, Chinese shipyards booked 74% of all newbuilding orders in 2024, with green vessel orders specifically accounting for 78.5% of the global market share. As a result, this manufacturing monopoly is increasingly influencing global procurement strategies and technical standards for methanol-powered fleets.

Simultaneously, the Diversification of Vessel Types Beyond Container Ships is accelerating as regulatory pressure expands to broader maritime sectors. While container lines initially led adoption, bulk carriers and cruise vessels are now aggressively integrating methanol propulsion to meet strict carbon intensity ratings. According to Marine Link in April 2025, this shift was evident as methanol led activity with 12 new orders in a single month, expanding specifically into cruise and car carrier segments. This sectoral broadening reduces reliance on a single vessel class, indicating a maturing technology stack suitable for varied operational profiles.

title: Wah Kwong takes China's ÔÇťfirstÔÇŁ dual-fuel methanol bunker vessel into managed fleet
description:
Hong Kong shipowner and ship manager Wah Kwong Maritime Transport Holdings Limited has brought the bunkering vessel Da Qing 268 under management, identifying it as ChinaÔÇÖs first dual-fuel methanol-powered bunker ship, according to Wah Kwong Maritime Transport Holdings Limited.
The company said the vessel is the fourth bunkering ship in its managed fleet.
ÔÇťWe are thrilled to welcome the ÔÇśDa Qing 268ÔÇÖ, ChinaÔÇÖs first dual-fuel methanol-powered bunkering vessel, to our managed fleet,ÔÇŁ Wah Kwong said.
Da Qing 268 is 109.9 metres long, has a deadweight of 7,500 tonnes and total tank capacity of 10,362 cubic metres.

text:
Hong Kong shipowner and ship manager Wah Kwong Maritime Transport Holdings Limited has brought the bunkering vessel Da Qing 268 under management, identifying it as ChinaÔÇÖs first dual-fuel methanol-powered bunker ship, according to Wah Kwong Maritime Transport Holdings Limited.

The company said the vessel is the fourth bunkering ship in its managed fleet. ÔÇťWe are thrilled to welcome the ÔÇśDa Qing 268ÔÇÖ, ChinaÔÇÖs first dual-fuel methanol-powered bunkering vessel, to our managed fleet,ÔÇŁ Wah Kwong said.

The move follows a ship-to-ship bunkering operation at Yantian port in Shenzhen on Jan. 21, when Da Qing 268 delivered 200 tonnes of green methanol to the container ship COSCO SHIPPING Carnation.
